College: College of Literature, Science, and the Arts (LSA) — Humanities
Description: Reading and interpretive proficiency in Ancient Greek language and literature — Homer, the tragedians, Plato — alongside the cultural and historical world of classical antiquity.
Classical and modern standard Arabic with the literature, history, and cultures of Arabic-speaking societies. Offered through Middle East Studies.
Material culture and art of the ancient Greek and Roman worlds through archaeological, textual, and art-historical methods with fieldwork opportunities.
Ancient Greek and Roman civilizations through literature, history, philosophy, religion, and art — conducted primarily in English translation.
Intensive study of both Latin and Greek language and literature, tracing the intellectual and literary traditions of the Greco-Roman world.
Literary works across national traditions and historical periods, often in original languages — translation theory, world literature, and comparative methodology.
Close reading, analysis, and historical interpretation of literature in the English language from medieval texts to contemporary writing; offers a creative writing track.
Moving-image media — film, television, digital and emerging platforms — through historical, theoretical, and cultural approaches, with hands-on production.
Advanced French language with French and Francophone literature, culture, film, and thought from the medieval period to the present.
Advanced German language alongside literature, intellectual history, and cultural production of German-speaking societies; includes Scandinavian Studies option.
The human past across civilizations and eras with emphasis on primary-source analysis and historical argumentation — subfields include U.S., European, Asian, and global history.
Visual arts — painting, sculpture, architecture, photography, and new media — in historical and cultural contexts from antiquity to the present.

Majors: Language, Literature, and Culture of Ancient Greece (A.B.)
Description: Integrated program in ancient Greek language and Hellenic civilization combining linguistic training with literary and cultural analysis.

Majors: Language, Literature, and Culture of Ancient Rome (A.B.)
Description: Integrated program in Latin language and Roman civilization covering literature, philosophy, history, and material culture.
Scientific study of human language — phonology, syntax, semantics, historical linguistics, and sociolinguistics. Sub-plans in computational linguistics and language and cognition.
Interdisciplinary study of culture, literature, history, and thought from roughly 500–1700 CE across European and global traditions.
Modern Greek language and the literature and culture of Greece from the Byzantine period to the present.
Fundamental questions in metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, political philosophy, and the philosophy of mind and language through major texts in Western and global traditions.
Polish language, literature, and culture. Housed in Slavic Languages and Literatures.
Advanced Portuguese with literature and culture from Portugal, Brazil, and other Lusophone societies.
Beliefs, practices, texts, and institutions of world religious traditions using historical, textual, and social-scientific approaches.
Advanced Russian language with Russian literature, intellectual history, and culture from medieval Kievan Rus through the present.
Languages (Swedish, Norwegian, Danish), literatures, and cultures of the Nordic countries. Housed in Germanic Languages and Literatures.
Slavic languages (Russian, Polish, Czech, etc.) alongside literary and cultural traditions of Slavic peoples with sub-concentrations in various languages.
Advanced Spanish language with literature and culture of Spain, Latin America, and the U.S. Latino diaspora.
Theory and practice of literary and cultural translation. Offered through Comparative Literature and Translation Studies.
Ukrainian language, literature, and history. Housed in Slavic Languages and Literatures.
Craft of writing across multiple genres and rhetorical situations. Offered through the Sweetland Center for Writing.

College: College of Literature, Science, and the Arts (LSA) — Social Sciences
Description: Historical, cultural, political, and intellectual traditions of African-descended peoples in Africa and the diaspora through interdisciplinary methods.
Interdisciplinary U.S. cultural history and society — sub-plans explore race, ethnicity, gender, region, and popular culture.
Human diversity through the four subfields — cultural, biological, linguistic, and archaeological anthropology.

Majors: Biopsychology, Cognition, and Neuroscience (BCN) (A.B. or B.S.)
Description: Interdisciplinary major housed in Psychology — biological bases of behavior, cognitive processes, and neural mechanisms. Distinct from the Neuroscience B.S.
Media industries, communication processes, political communication, media effects, and media culture across social-scientific and humanistic approaches.
Interdisciplinary study of mind and intelligence — linguistics, psychology, philosophy, computer science, and neuroscience. Weinberg Institute for Cognitive Science.
Microeconomic and macroeconomic theory, econometrics, and applied economics across labor, industrial organization, and international trade.
Gender as it intersects with health, medicine, and social structures. Offered through the Women's and Gender Studies program.
Human evolutionary biology, primatology, paleoanthropology, and behavioral ecology. Offered through Anthropology.
Area studies, language, economics, political science, and history applied to global affairs. Regional or thematic sub-plans (e.g., International Security; Political Economy & Development).
Advanced Japanese language with history, culture, literature, and contemporary society of Japan. Housed in Asian Languages and Cultures.
Jewish civilization, history, religious thought, and cultural production from antiquity through the present in global contexts.
Interdisciplinary history, politics, cultures, and literature of Latin America and the Caribbean. International Institute.
History, cultures, and social experiences of Latino communities in the United States. Housed in American Culture.
The law in its social context — how legal institutions shape and are shaped by structures of inequality. Housed in Sociology.
Sexuality, gender identity, queer theory, and LGBTQ+ history and culture. Women's and Gender Studies.
Health, illness, and medicine from anthropological perspectives — culture, society, and biology in human health experience.
History, politics, languages, and cultures of the Middle East and North Africa. Center for Middle Eastern and North African Studies.
Interdisciplinary European history, politics, culture, and thought from the early modern period to the present. International Institute.

Majors: Modern Middle Eastern and North African Studies (A.B.)
Description: Language-based study of contemporary politics, cultures, and societies of the MENA region. International Institute.
Competitive-admissions interdisciplinary major drawing on economics, sociology, and psychology — how organizations function and questions of leadership and change.
Competitive-admissions interdisciplinary major integrating analytic philosophy, political science, and formal economic reasoning.
Political institutions, processes, and behavior across American politics, comparative politics, international relations, and political theory.
Scientific study of human and animal behavior and mental processes — developmental, social, clinical, personality, cognitive, and neuroscience perspectives.

Majors: Quantitative Methods in the Social Sciences (QMSS) (A.B.)
Description: Statistical and computational methods for social science research combined with a substantive social science focus.
Interdisciplinary examination of the relationships among science, technology, and society.
Causes and consequences of economic inequality and class stratification. Offered through Women's and Gender Studies.
Social structures, institutions, and processes — inequality, culture, organizations, and social movements using quantitative and qualitative methods.
Interdisciplinary study of cities and metropolitan regions — urban history, planning, policy, and social dynamics.
Interdisciplinary program examining gender, sexuality, race, and other axes of identity and power using feminist and critical frameworks.

College: College of Literature, Science, and the Arts (LSA) — Natural Sciences & Mathematics
Description: The universe from planets and stars to galaxies and cosmology, integrating physics and mathematics with observational astronomy.
Chemical processes underlying living organisms — protein structure and function, metabolism, molecular biology, and enzyme mechanisms with substantial lab work.
Broad survey of biological sciences — cell biology, genetics, ecology, evolution, and physiology.

Majors: Biology, Health, and Society (BHS) (A.B. or B.S.)
Description: Interdisciplinary biological, social, and policy dimensions of health and medicine — fewer lab credits than core biology majors.
Chemistry and biology at the molecular level — nucleic acid biochemistry, protein structure, and molecular cellular mechanisms. Housed in Chemistry.
Physics and mathematics applied to biological problems at molecular, cellular, and systems levels.
Interdisciplinary major at the boundary of chemistry and biology — medicinal chemistry, chemical genetics, and biochemical analysis. Chemistry department.
Inorganic, organic, physical, and analytical chemistry. The B.S. Chem. is ACS-certified with additional coursework and laboratory hours.
LSA/Engineering joint Computer Science program — algorithms, data structures, software systems, and theory in a liberal arts context. Distinct from the BSE in CS.
Statistics, computing, and domain knowledge for analyzing complex datasets. Offered through the Statistics department.

Majors: Earth and Environmental Sciences (A.B. or B.S.)
Description: Physical, chemical, and biological processes shaping the Earth — geology, hydrology, atmospheric science, and environmental geochemistry. Sub-plans include Oceanography and Paleontology.

Majors: Ecology, Evolution, and Biodiversity (EEB) (A.B. or B.S.)
Description: Ecological interactions, evolutionary processes, and the diversity of life — field biology, population genetics, and conservation biology.
Interdisciplinary major combining computing, statistics, and social science to analyze and design information systems. Administered by Statistics.
Rigorous astronomy and physics with additional study in mathematics or a related field — deeper quantitative preparation than the standard Astronomy major.
Physics combined with a cognate area such as mathematics, biophysics, or computation.
Pure and applied mathematics — analysis, algebra, topology, and probability. Sub-plans in actuarial mathematics and mathematical finance.
Microorganisms — bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protozoa — including genetics, immunology, ecology, and roles in medicine and industry.
Molecular and cellular mechanisms of development, gene regulation, and cell signaling — designed for research or graduate study.
Structure and function of the nervous system from molecular and cellular levels to systems and behavior. Undergraduate Program in Neuroscience.
Classical and modern physics — mechanics, electromagnetism, quantum mechanics, and statistical physics.
Plants at all levels — molecular, cellular, organismal, and ecological — with emphasis on evolution, genetics, and ecology.
Synthesis, properties, and applications of polymers and macromolecules. Housed in Chemistry.
Statistical theory, data analysis, probability, and computational methods. Sub-plans include applied statistics.
Joint LSA/Biomedical Engineering program — cell/molecular biology with biomedical engineering coursework.

Majors: Arts and Ideas in the Humanities (A.B.)
Description: RC-administered interdisciplinary major (open to all LSA students) combining literature, visual arts, music, and drama with global cultural attention — analytical, historical, and studio skills.
RC major combining disciplined creative writing practice (fiction, poetry, creative nonfiction, digital storytelling) with rigorous literary study — workshops and tutorials.
RC major treating drama as a full academic discipline — theatrical history and criticism combined with practical performance and production.
RC interdisciplinary social-sciences major integrating anthropology, economics, geography, history, political science, psychology, and sociology with community-engaged learning.
Pre-professional pathway combining natural sciences and pharmacy sciences for direct entry to the PharmD program. Administered jointly with the College of Pharmacy.

Jointly administered by LSA and SEAS through the Program in the Environment (PitE) — natural science, social science, and humanities perspectives. Specialization areas: Environmental Sciences; Environmental Social Sciences; Culture and Environment.
Pathway within the School of Information's offerings combining data analysis and UX/design methods with information science theory.
Interdisciplinary program on the design, use, and societal impacts of social media platforms. School of Information with LSA.
Human-centered design of digital products and services. Offered through the School of Information for LSA cross-enrollees.
Designed for students creating their own interdisciplinary program of study within LSA.
Aeronautics (aircraft design and atmospheric flight) and astronautics (spacecraft design and space exploration) — aerodynamics, propulsion, structures, and control. ABET-accredited.
Engineering applied to building design and construction — structural systems, HVAC, electrical, and integration with architecture. ABET-accredited.
Engineering integrated with life sciences for medical devices, diagnostics, and therapies — biomechanics, biomaterials, bioimaging, neural engineering. ABET-accredited.
Large-scale chemical and biochemical processes — thermodynamics, reaction kinetics, transport phenomena, and process design. ABET-accredited.
Structural, geotechnical, hydraulic, transportation, and construction engineering for design and management of the built environment. ABET-accredited.
Earth's atmosphere and climate system — meteorology, climatology, atmospheric dynamics, and remote sensing. Administered by CLaSP.
Electrical engineering and computer science combined — hardware-software integration, embedded systems, digital design, and architecture. ABET-accredited.
Algorithms, data structures, software systems, programming languages, and theoretical CS. Offered through EECS; distinct from the LSA CS major.
Interdisciplinary engineering major combining statistics, machine learning, and computational methods. Offered through EECS.
Circuits, electronics, signals and systems, electromagnetics, control, power systems, and communications. ABET-accredited.
Rigorous mathematical physics with engineering applications. Administered by Nuclear Engineering & Radiological Sciences.
Air, water, and soil quality engineering — water treatment, environmental fluid dynamics, earth systems, and environmental policy. ABET-accredited.
Engineering tools and systems thinking for global health challenges in low-resource settings — disease surveillance, water/sanitation systems, and health technologies.
IOE — design and optimization of complex human–machine systems using operations research, statistics, ergonomics, and systems analysis. ABET-accredited.
Structure, properties, processing, and performance of metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites. ABET-accredited.
Thermal and fluid sciences, solid mechanics and materials, dynamics and controls, and design. ABET-accredited.
Ship and ocean structure design, stability, hydrodynamics, and propulsion systems. ABET-accredited.
Nuclear reactions, radiation, plasma physics — energy generation, medical imaging, radiation safety, and national security. ABET-accredited.

Interdisciplinary major combining ME, EE, and CS for autonomous machines that sense, reason, and act. Michigan's Robotics Institute offers one of the first standalone Robotics BSE programs in the U.S.
Space environment, spacecraft systems, and space physics — heliophysics, magnetospheric dynamics, and mission design. Administered by CLaSP.

College: Stephen M. Ross School of Business
Description: Four-year professional degree covering all core business disciplines — financial and managerial accounting, applied economics, financial management, marketing, business analytics and statistics, operations, business law and ethics, behavioral theory, communication, and strategy. Informal areas of study: Accounting; Business Communication; Business Economics and Public Policy; Business Law; Entrepreneurship; Finance and Banking; Management and Organizations; Marketing; Nonprofit and Social Impact; Sales; Strategy; Supply Chain Management; Technology and Operations.

College: Penny W. Stamps School of Art & Design
Description: Studio-intensive professional degree — roughly 75% studio coursework. Mastery of technique across graphic design, illustration, animation, ceramics, painting, printmaking, textile design, and product design within an integrated Art & Design framework.

Liberal-arts degree with roughly half studio and half academic coursework — for students who plan graduate professional school in a non-art field or want broader intellectual study alongside studio practice.

College: Taubman College of Architecture and Urban Planning
Description: Four-year pre-professional degree centered on design studios with architectural history and theory, building technology, structures, environmental systems, and professional practice. Pathway to the M.Arch.

Combines urbanism, technology, and design — Cities Intensive foundational courses, three coding courses, and four sequential design studios at the intersection of digital and physical urban environments.
Data science, statistical analysis, and data visualization, bridging technical and organizational roles in information systems.
Human-centered design of interactive products and services — UX research methods, prototyping, and usability evaluation.
Physiological and biomechanical responses to exercise and physical activity for clinical settings, cardiac rehabilitation, fitness assessment, and health promotion.
Human movement — biomechanics, motor behavior, exercise physiology, and neuroscience. Optional Intraoperative Neuromonitoring concentration. Preparation for PT/OT, research, medicine, and athletics.
Business and organizational dimensions of sport — marketing, finance, law, facility management, and leadership for professional sport, collegiate athletics, and sport industries.
Craft of musical composition across orchestral, chamber, electroacoustic, and experimental music with private lessons and ensemble performance.
Performance and composition in jazz and contemporary improvised music — improvisation, arranging, theory, and ensemble leadership.
K–12 choral music teacher certification combining performance training with pedagogy, conducting, and student teaching.
K–12 instrumental (band/orchestra) teacher certification combining performance and pedagogy.
Tonal harmony, counterpoint, post-tonal analysis, and analytical tools for understanding music's structural logic.
Historical and cultural study of music — repertoire, composers, performance practice, and social contexts from antiquity to the present.
Recording studio, sound design, music technology, and music production combined with computer science, acoustics, and media.
Performance training for trumpet, horn, trombone, tuba — private lessons, chamber music, and large ensemble performance.
Piano accompanying, chamber music coaching, and vocal/instrumental collaboration.
Classical organ performance and repertoire — sacred music, improvisation, and service playing.
Solo piano performance — technique, repertoire, and music history.
Performance training for violin, viola, cello, bass, guitar, harp — private lessons, chamber, and orchestral performance.
Classical voice — soprano, mezzo-soprano, tenor, baritone, bass — opera studio, art song, and diction.
Woodwind and percussion performance — technique, solo repertoire, and chamber/orchestral performance.

Majors: Performance and Music Education (B.M. Double Major)
Description: Combined degree for intensive performance training and teacher certification — meets requirements of both programs.
Classical performance (piano, strings, winds, percussion, organ, voice) with substantial academic component outside SMTD.
Jazz performance with broad liberal arts curriculum — professional jazz preparation plus significant academic engagement.
Flexible professional degree integrating music with coursework across the University tailored to students' musical and non-artistic interests.
Conservatory-style training in theatrical acting — voice, movement, script analysis, and scene study. Among the most selective programs at U-M.
Conservatory-level training in ballet, modern, jazz, and contemporary dance — dance history, choreography, and performance practice.
Theatrical directing — script analysis, rehearsal techniques, production management, and collaborative leadership.
Joint SMTD/Stamps program — performance at the intersection of music, theatre, visual art, and new media.
Intensive jazz performance with improvisation, arranging, and ensemble leadership; studio art orientation distinct from the B.M. in Jazz.
Professional training combining acting, singing, and dance for the musical theatre stage. One of the most prestigious and selective musical theatre programs in the U.S.
Studio-intensive design and production aspects of sound, lighting, and technology in live and recorded performance — distinct from the B.M. in PAT.
Scenic, costume, lighting, and sound design plus technical direction for theatrical production with studio projects and production assignments.
Liberal arts theatre degree — comprehensive theatre education alongside substantial academic work for stage management, arts administration, dramaturgy, or teaching.
Liberal arts music degree — broad musical knowledge in theory, history, and performance without performance-intensive B.M. requirements.
Acoustics, physics of sound, sound engineering, computer science, and sound recording. Higher academic entry requirements than other SMTD programs.

Four-year professional nursing degree integrating theory, clinical practice, and evidence-based care across the lifespan with substantial hospital and community placements. Graduates are eligible to sit for the NCLEX-RN.

Majors: Learning, Equity, and Problem Solving for the Public Good (LEAPS) (B.A.)
Description: First-of-its-kind four-year undergraduate program in education leadership with a first-year living-learning experience on the Marygrove campus in Detroit. Trains 'learning leaders' for systemic change.

Majors: Educator Preparation Program (B.A./B.S. with Teacher Certification)
Description: Professional preparation for certified K–12 teachers. Elementary candidates earn a B.A. in Education from Marsal; secondary candidates remain in their home unit (e.g., LSA, Engineering) and complete certification through Marsal.

Public health challenges through culture, social justice, ethics, policy, and global health systems. Requires fourth-year foreign-language proficiency. Health education/promotion, public health administration, and nonprofit/government careers.

Science-based public health — biological, environmental, and epidemiological dimensions of disease. Requires public health biology and pathophysiology. Preparation for clinical health professions, epidemiology, and environmental health.

College: Gerald R. Ford School of Public Policy
Description: Two-year upper-level program — analytical skills for examining policy problems, understanding political and institutional stakes, and designing solutions. Core in public policy, economics, statistics, and political science with student-selected policy focus electives.

Jointly conferred with LSA through the Program in the Environment (PitE). Interdisciplinary perspective integrating natural sciences, social sciences, and humanities. Specializations: Environmental Sciences; Environmental Social Sciences; Culture and Environment. Field study or supervised internship required.
